quiz.title: Geometry: End-of-Year Review — Area and Volume
quiz.subtitle: Unit 12 Quiz 9: Comprehensive Review of Units 10 and 11 (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 12.9 End-of-Year Review: Area and Volume

questions.question: A trapezoid has bases of $14$ cm and $22$ cm, and a height of $9$ cm. What is its area?
questions.explanation: Area $= \frac{1}{2}h(b_1 + b_2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times (14 + 22) = 4.5 \times 36 = 162$ cm$^2$. The average of the bases is $18$ cm, multiplied by height $9$ cm gives $162$ cm$^2$.

questions.question: A circle has diameter $18$ inches. A sector with central angle $80^\circ$ is shaded. What is the area of the shaded sector, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Radius $= 9$ in. Full circle area $= \pi(9^2) = 81\pi \approx 254.47$ in$^2$. Sector area $= \frac{80}{360} \times 81\pi = \frac{2}{9} \times 81\pi = 18\pi \approx 56.5$ in$^2$.

questions.question: A regular hexagon has side length $6$ m and apothem $3\sqrt{3}$ m. What is its exact area?
A cylinder has radius $4$ cm and height $15$ cm. What is its volume in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: $V = \pi r^2 h = \pi(4^2)(15) = \pi(16)(15) = 240\pi$ cm$^3$. The lateral surface area would be $2\pi(4)(15) = 120\pi$ cm$^2$, but volume uses base area $\times$ height.

questions.question: A cone has radius $6$ ft and vertical height $8$ ft. What is its volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3}\pi(36)(8) = \frac{1}{3}\pi(288) = 96\pi \approx 301.6$ ft$^3$. The slant height would be $\sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = 10$ ft, but volume uses vertical height, not slant height.

questions.question: A sphere has radius $9$ cm. What is its surface area in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: $SA = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi(9^2) = 4\pi(81) = 324\pi$ cm$^2$. The volume would be $\frac{4}{3}\pi(9^3) = 972\pi$ cm$^3$. Surface area is exactly four times the area of a great circle.

questions.question: A square pyramid has base side $10$ in and slant height $13$ in. What is its total surface area?
questions.explanation: Perimeter $= 4 \times 10 = 40$ in. Lateral area $= \frac{1}{2}P\ell = \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 13 = 260$ in$^2$. Base area $= 10^2 = 100$ in$^2$. Total $= 260 + 100 = 360$ in$^2$.

questions.question: Two similar cylinders have radii in the ratio $2:5$. The smaller cylinder has volume $32\pi$ cm$^3$. What is the larger cylinder's volume?
questions.explanation: Scale factor $k = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$. Volume scales by $k^3 = (2.5)^3 = 15.625$. Larger volume $= 32\pi \times 15.625 = 500\pi$ cm$^3$. Equivalently: $k^3 = \frac{125}{8}$, so $V_{\text{large}} = 32\pi \times \frac{125}{8} = 4\pi \times 125 = 500\pi$.

questions.question: A composite solid consists of a hemisphere on top of a cylinder. Both have radius $3$ m. The cylinder height is $7$ m. What is the total volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Cylinder: $V = \pi(3^2)(7) = 63\pi$ m$^3$. Hemisphere: $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi(3^3) = 18\pi$ m$^3$. Total $= 63\pi + 18\pi = 81\pi \approx 254.5$ m$^3$.

questions.question: A solid iron sphere has radius $5$ cm. Iron has density $7.8$ g/cm$^3$. What is the mass of the sphere, rounded to the nearest gram?
questions.explanation: Volume $= \frac{4}{3}\pi(5^3) = \frac{4}{3}\pi(125) = \frac{500\pi}{3} \approx 523.60$ cm$^3$. Mass $= D \times V = 7.8 \times 523.60 \approx 4{,}084.1 \approx 4{,}084$ grams (about $4.1$ kg).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the core concepts of Units 10 and 11: area of trapezoids, regular polygons, circles and sectors; surface area and volume of cylinders, cones, pyramids, spheres, and composite solids; similar solids; and density applications.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review: (1) Trapezoid area: $\frac{1}{2}h(b_1 + b_2)$, (2) Regular polygon: $\frac{1}{2}aP$, (3) Cone volume: $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ (vertical height, not slant), (4) Sphere: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, $SA = 4\pi r^2$, (5) Similar solids: area scales by $k^2$, volume by $k^3$.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) using the correct height — vertical for volume, slant for pyramid/cone surface area, (2) the $\frac{1}{3}$ factor for pyramids and cones, (3) composite volumes just add, composite surface areas require subtracting hidden faces, (4) density: $m = DV$, $V = \frac{m}{D}$.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Unit 10: area of quadrilaterals, triangles, regular polygons ($A = \frac{1}{2}aP$), circles and sectors, composite figures, geometric probability, and optimization. Unit 11: surface area and volume of prisms/cylinders ($V = Bh$), pyramids/cones ($V = \frac{1}{3}Bh$), spheres ($V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, $SA = 4\pi r^2$), similar solids ($k$, $k^2$, $k^3$), and density.
